clues: Since 1988, a professor at Michigan State University has tracked 65,000 generations of this
Widely used to produce recombinant human insulin, this is better known for transmission through contaminated food
Initially called Bacterium coli commune, the name was changed in 1919 to honor its discoverer
Amonst the most prescribed medications in the US, this medication is known for its quick onset of action within 5-10 minutes
A mixture of two enantiomers, this medication can cause hypokalemia and tremors
Current delivery technology of this medication was largely inspired by a teenager asking why her medication wasn't in a spray can like hairspray
Hallmark symptoms of this disease were first described by (and named after) a French physician in Paris in 1877. At that time, this disease had a mortality of more than 50%
Affecting men and women equally, severe cases lead to increased pressures causing bacterial translocation into the bloodstream and bacteremia
Classically associated with a triad of symptoms, severe cases may present with a pentad
Few know the origins of this are from a cattle disease caused by spoiled sweet clover
A military recruit attempted suicide using this medication but survived, playing a pivotal role in FDA approval of this for human use
You may have never considered the origin of the name, but it's actually derived from the Wisconsin Alumni research foundation that supported the original research
Discovered in the 17th century by an italian scientist, these are found only in mammals
With extremely thin walls, these function primarily by diffusion
Beginning to develop around day 22 in utero, adults have roughly 300-400 million of these with a total surface area of approximately a tennis court
